questions.scenario: Public users can reach the API, but checkout fails only when writing to SQL. Which first check is most relevant?

options.text: Private endpoint, private DNS, SQL firewall, and app identity for SQL.

questions.explanation: The public path works. The failure is in the private data path, so SQL private access and permissions are the right first checks.

questions.scenario: A teammate wants to enable public network access on SQL during the incident. What should the reviewer ask first?

options.text: Which private path evidence failed, and is there a narrower repair that preserves private access?
Can the setting be enabled quickly from the portal?
Will a public endpoint make the dashboard greener?
Can the app retry enough times to hide the change?

questions.explanation: Opening public access changes the exposure model. The reviewer should ask for evidence and a narrower private-path fix first.

questions.scenario: Which symptoms point at private DNS instead of public entry?

options.text: The API logs show SQL hostname resolution to a public address.
The browser never reaches Application Gateway.
The private endpoint has IP `10.30.40.7`, but the app connects to a different SQL IP.
Only production fails, and production uses a different private DNS zone link.

questions.explanation: Private DNS controls how the app resolves the data-service name. Browser entry failure belongs earlier in the public path.
